summary: Gruber analyzes whether existing iPhone owners should upgrade to the new iPhone 3G, concluding it's a tough sell. The only meaningful hardware differences are 3G networking, GPS, and a new case design — the CPU, camera, and screen are essentially unchanged. He notes that the original iPhone will receive the same 2.0 OS update with performance and battery improvements for free. The 3G radio actually worsens battery life significantly for voice calls, and the upgrade cost plus higher monthly data fees make it a questionable value proposition for current owners.
key_points: The iPhone 3G's internal components — CPU, GPU, camera, and screen — are essentially unchanged from the original iPhone
The free iPhone OS 2.0 update will bring performance and battery-life improvements to original iPhone owners, narrowing the gap
3G networking cuts talk-time battery life in half (5 vs. 10 hours) compared to 2G, and there's no option to use 3G for data only
The real upgrade cost is $299 for the 16GB model plus $15/month extra for the new data plan, just for faster cellular data and GPS
money_quotes: The old iPhone is still, by far, the most powerful handheld computing device in the world.
Most iPhone owners have the common sense not to buy a new phone less than a year after spending $400 (or more) on their present one.
For the rest of us who lack such common sense and are considering upgrading — yours truly included — it's not a sure thing.
An iPhone 3G with 3G turned off is now just an iPhone with GPS.
With faster networking, the network is actually used for shorter durations, whereas with voice calls 3G doesn't make your conversation go faster.
So what it boils down to is whether you think it's worth $199 up front — plus $15 extra per month for the new data plan with SMS — for faster cell network data.
key_insight: The iPhone 3G is a clear win for new buyers but a hard sell for existing owners, since the only real upgrades are 3G and GPS while the free OS update narrows the gap considerably.
